title: Electronic signatures — control theme 5
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the FDA Part 11 programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with FDA Part 11 control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for §11.200.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that FDA Part 11 §11.200 (Electronic signatures) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.17.10: Electronic signatures — control theme 5.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.17.10' running on LIMS, MES, ELN, CDS, QMS/ERP validation logs, Windows security for GxP workstations, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Electronic signatures — control theme 5' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper FDA Part 11 control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the FDA Part 11 control objective.

dataSources: LIMS, MES, ELN, CDS, QMS/ERP validation logs, Windows security for GxP workstations
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk DB Connect (2686), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=gxp sourcetype=eln:signature earliest=-30d
| eval uc_tag="22.17.10"
| transaction signer_id maxspan=120s
| eval steps=mvcount(signature_step)
| where steps < 3 AND match(document_type,"(?i)release")
description: Supports Part 11 technical controls evidence by correlating validated system audit trails with identity and change records.
value: Release workflows that skip mandated signature steps can ship product or reports without the QC and QA gates the business and regulators rely on.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=gxp`.
visualization: Time chart, Table, Single value. Key fields: `steps`, `uc_tag`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `gxp` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§11.200`. The control objective is: "Evidence that FDA Part 11 §11.200 (Electronic signatures) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.17.10: Electronic signatures — control theme 5." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `gxp` under `eln:signature`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Electronic signatures — control theme 5," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the FDA Part 11 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| transaction` to group events that share a correlation identifier into a single result row. Transactions are analytically powerful but operationally expensive: Splunk must buffer events in memory until the transaction closes, which caps the practical event volume (default `maxevents=1000` per transaction, `maxopentxn=10000` concurrent transactions).

Scale warning: for compliance searches that must cover multi-day spans with potentially tens of thousands of open transactions, `| transaction` will either truncate or OOM. Prefer `| stats earliest(...) latest(...)` grouped by the same correlation ID — it produces the same analytical result with no memory cap and 5-20x better performance.

When `| transaction` is the right choice: when you need the event sequence preserved (e.g. detecting a specific ordering of events like 'login -> elevate -> access -> logout'), when you need `maxpause` or `maxspan` constraints between events, or when you need `startswith`/`endswith` markers. If you only need min/max of a field across the group, always use `| stats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.17.10: Electronic signatures — control theme 5]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.17.10,reg=FDA_Part_11
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=gxp sourcetype=eln:signature earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `steps`, `uc_tag`. Run:

```spl
index=gxp sourcetype=eln:signature earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table steps, uc_tag
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `steps`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(steps) as p50 perc90(steps) as p90 perc99(steps) as p99 max(steps) as hi
```

The current threshold `3` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.17.10: FDA Part 11 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (the key fields):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'FDA Part 11 current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**FDA Part 11-specific failure modes:**

• Predicate rule context: 21 CFR Part 11 overlays a predicate rule (e.g. 21 CFR Part 211 for pharma GMP). Detections must reference the predicate rule — Part 11 alone doesn't define what must be recorded, only how electronic records are treated.

• Signature semantics: Part 11 distinguishes between 'electronic signature' (legally equivalent to wet ink) and 'electronic approval.' Detections on signature integrity must identify WHICH signature type an event represents; generic 'approval recorded' is not evidence of 11-compliant signing.

• Audit trail independence (§11.10(e)): the audit trail must be independent of operator action. A detection measuring 'audit trail presence' must test for independence — if operators can modify the audit trail, the system fails Part 11 regardless of what events were captured.

• Time source authority: §11.10(d) requires operator actions to be 'time-stamped.' The time source must be documented and auditable (not the user's desktop clock). Detections must verify events use the authoritative time source; clock drift >5 seconds is a deviation.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show that electronic signatures in your validated lab and manufacturing systems line up with how you said they would work, so food and drug inspections can trust the record.
